# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~1 billion

**Fraction with access to windows (any opening to look through):**
- Europe (~200M): ~180M had windows
- China (~330M): ~250M
- India/S. Asia (~170M): ~100M
- Japan (~30M): ~25M
- Americas, Middle East, Africa, SE Asia: ~105M combined
- **Total: ~660 million people**

**Average daily window-gazing time:**
- ~80% rural/agricultural workers: outdoors most of the day; maybe ~1 min/day (quick weather check, glance when indoors)
- ~15% urban residents/craftspeople: ~5–7 min/day
- ~5% wealthy/elderly/housebound: ~15 min/day
- **Weighted average: ~2.5 minutes/person/day**

## Calculation

$$660{,}000{,}000 \text{ people} \times 2.5 \text{ min/day} \times 365 \text{ days} = 602{,}250{,}000{,}000 \text{ person-minutes}$$

$$\frac{602{,}250{,}000{,}000}{1{,}440 \text{ min/day}} \approx 418{,}000{,}000 \text{ person-days}$$

## Adjustments & Sanity Check
- Many windows were small, shuttered in winter, or poor-quality glass → slightly reduces effective gazing time
- Children under ~3 contribute negligibly
- Nights are long with no electric light; limited indoor visibility through windows after dark

These push the estimate slightly downward.

## My estimate: **380,000,000 person-days**

# Estimating Total Human-Days Spent Looking Through Windows in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~1 billion

**Fraction with meaningful window access:**
| Region | Population | Window access rate | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|
| Europe | ~200M | ~80% | Even peasant cottages had small glass windows |
| China | ~330M | ~50% | Paper/lattice windows widespread |
| Indian subcontinent | ~200M | ~30% | Many simple dwellings, but openings existed |
| Americas | ~30M | ~35% | Mixed colonial/indigenous |
| Middle East/N. Africa | ~40M | ~40% | Traditional architecture included windows |
| Sub-Saharan Africa | ~100M | ~12% | Limited window-style openings |
| Rest of world | ~100M | ~30% | Variable |

**Effective population with window access: ~450 million**

**Average daily window-gazing time (~2.5–3 min/day):**
- ~85% were rural agricultural workers spending most daylight hours **outside**; quick weather checks, brief glances during meals ≈ 1–2 min/day
- Urban/indoor workers: ~5–8 min/day
- Elderly/infirm/children indoors: ~5–15 min/day
- Evenings were dark (no electric light), reducing window-gazing to near zero after sunset

**Weighted average: ~3 minutes/day**

## Calculation

$$450{,}000{,}000 \text{ people} \times 365 \text{ days} \times 3 \text{ min/day} = 492.75 \text{ billion person-minutes}$$

$$\frac{492{,}750{,}000{,}000}{1{,}440 \text{ min/day}} \approx 342{,}000{,}000 \text{ person-days}$$

## My estimate: **350,000,000 person-days**

- **Americas + rest of world (~170M):** ~50%

**Average daily window-gazing time (~3.5 min/day):**
- ~60% outdoor laborers/farmers (awake hours spent outside): ~1 min/day
- ~25% moderate indoor time (domestic workers, craftsmen, doing tasks near windows for light): ~5 min/day
- ~15% mostly indoor (elderly, infirm, wealthy, young children in winter): ~10 min/day
- Weighted average: 0.6×1 + 0.25×5 + 0.15×10 = **3.35 min/day ≈ 3.5 min/day**

$$500{,}000{,}000 \text{ people} \times 365 \text{ days} \times \frac{3.5 \text{ min}}{1440 \text{ min/day}} \approx 443{,}600{,}000 \text{ person-days}$$

## My estimate: **450,000,000 person-days**

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~1 billion

**Fraction with meaningful window access:**
| Region | Population | % with window access | People with access |
|--------|-----------|---------------------|-------------------|
| Europe | 200M | ~60% | 120M |
| China | 330M | ~15–20% | 55M |
| India | 200M | ~12% | 25M |
| Rest of Asia | 165M | ~8% | 13M |
| Americas | 30M | ~30% | 9M |
| Africa | 100M | ~5% | 5M |
| **Total** | | | **~225–275M** |

I use **~275 million** people with regular access to a transparent/open window.

**Average daily window-gazing time:**
- Most people (~80%) were agricultural laborers spending daylight hours *outdoors* — perhaps 1–3 minutes of window glancing per day.
- Urban workers/artisans near windows: ~8–10 min/day.
- Wealthy/elderly/children indoors: ~15–20 min/day.
- **Weighted average: ~4–5 minutes/day** for those with access.

## Calculation

$$275{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times \frac{4.5 \;\text{min}}{1440 \;\text{min/day}} \times 365 \;\text{days} \approx 314{,}000{,}000 \;\text{person-days}$$

### Sanity check
That's ~860,000 full-time-equivalent window-gazers — about 0.09% of the global population. Given how rare indoor leisure was in 1800, this feels right.

## My estimate

**350,000,000 days**

(I round slightly upward from the calculation to account for people in warmer climates looking through open shutters/doorways that I may have undercounted.)

# Estimating Total Person-Days Spent Looking Through Windows in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~1 billion

**Fraction with meaningful window access:**
- **Europe (~200M):** Glass windows were widespread in Western/Central Europe; ~70% had access → ~140M
- **China (~300M):** Paper/lattice windows were common in traditional architecture; ~30% had something to look *through* → ~90M
- **India (~200M), Africa (~100M), etc.:** Most dwellings had minimal or no window openings suited for gazing → ~10-15% → ~40M
- **Americas (~25M), Ottoman/Middle East (~30M):** Mixed → ~30% → ~15M
- **Other regions:** ~15M

**Total with window access: ~450 million people**

**Average daily window-gazing time:**
- ~80% were agricultural laborers spending most daylight hours **outdoors** → ~1–2 min/day
- ~15% urban/indoor workers, homemakers, craftspeople → ~5 min/day
- ~5% wealthy, elderly, sick, young children indoors → ~10–15 min/day
- **Weighted average: ~3 minutes/day** (= 3/1440 of a day ≈ 0.00208 days)

## Calculation

$$450{,}000{,}000 \;\times\; 365 \;\times\; \frac{3}{1440}$$

$$= 450{,}000{,}000 \;\times\; 0.7604 \;\approx\; 342{,}000{,}000$$

## My estimate: **340,000,000 person-days**

This reflects the reality that in 1800 the vast majority of humanity worked outdoors, windows were small and scarce outside of Europe, and deliberate "gazing through a window" was a brief incidental activity rather than a pastime.

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~1 billion

**People with access to windows (glass or open/shuttered):**
- Europe (~200M): ~70% had glass windows → ~140M
- North America (~7M): ~5M with glass windows
- Rest of world (~800M): Most dwellings lacked glass, but many had open/shuttered window openings in warmer climates. Wealthy/urban populations in China, India, Ottoman Empire, Japan had some form of window. Perhaps ~150M had a usable window opening.
- **Total: ~300 million people**

**Average daily time spent "looking through a window":**
- The vast majority were agricultural laborers spending most daylight hours outdoors.
- Indoor time was often in dim conditions (candles expensive), so people near windows might glance out.
- Active gazing (checking weather, watching the street, idle moments) averages perhaps **5 minutes/day** across all window-havers — accounting for the mix of elderly/sick/wealthy who gaze more and field workers who barely do.

## Calculation

$$300{,}000{,}000 \text{ people} \times 365 \text{ days} \times \frac{5}{1440} \text{ (fraction of day)}$$

$$= 300{,}000{,}000 \times 1.267 \approx 380{,}000{,}000 \text{ person-days}$$

## My Estimate

**380,000,000 days**

This accounts for the fact that in 1800, most of humanity was rural, spent the bulk of daylight outdoors, and a large share of the global population (Sub-Saharan Africa, rural Asia, indigenous populations) had limited or no window access. Even 5 minutes/day is arguably generous for the average person with window access.

- The vast majority were agricultural laborers, spending most daylight hours *outside*

- Winter months drove more indoor time in northern latitudes; summer less so
- **Estimate: ~2.5 minutes/day averaged across all people with window access**

## Calculation

$$325{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times 365 \;\text{days} \times \frac{2.5}{1440} \;\text{(fraction of a day)} \approx 206{,}000{,}000 \;\text{person-days}$$

**200,000,000 person-days**

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~1 billion

**Fraction with access to windows (broadly defined, including unglazed openings/paper windows):**

| Region | Population | % with window access |
|---|---|---|
| Europe | ~200M | ~80% |
| China | ~300M | ~60% (paper windows common) |
| India/S. Asia | ~200M | ~40% |
| Americas | ~30M | ~50% |
| Africa | ~100M | ~20% |
| Rest of world | ~130M | ~35% |

**Weighted total with access: ~500 million people**

**Average daily time spent looking through a window:**
- 1800 was overwhelmingly agrarian — most people spent daylight hours **outdoors**
- Indoor time during daylight was limited (meals, chores, rest)
- Windows were often small; evenings were dark (no outdoor lighting to see through windows)
- European homemakers/craftspeople: ~10 min/day
- Outdoor workers everywhere: ~2–3 min/day
- Children/elderly: ~5 min/day

**Weighted average across those with access: ~3.5 minutes/day**

## Calculation

$$500{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times \frac{3.5}{1440}\;\text{days} \times 365\;\text{days/year}$$

$$= 500{,}000{,}000 \times 0.000243 \times 365 \approx \boxed{450{,}000{,}000 \text{ person-days}}$$

## My Point Estimate: **450,000,000 days**

This reflects that the vast majority of humanity in 1800 lived agrarian lives spent mostly outdoors, with modest dwellings and small or absent windows, limiting cumulative gazing time to just a few minutes per day on average.

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~1 billion

- **Sub-Saharan Africa (~70M):** Traditional dwellings often lacked formal windows → ~15% → 10M
- **Americas, SE Asia, other (~140M):** Mixed → ~30% → 42M

**Total with window access: ~480 million** → I'll conservatively round to **400 million** (accounting for shuttered/paper windows that are hard to look through, people away from home, etc.)

**Average daily window-gazing time:**
Most people in 1800 were agricultural laborers spending the vast majority of daylight hours **outdoors**. Indoor time was for sleeping, meals, and some crafts. Dedicated window-looking (checking weather, watching for visitors, idle gazing) likely averaged **~3 minutes/day** for those with access — some much more (elderly, wealthy, homebound), most much less.

## Calculation

$$400{,}000{,}000 \text{ people} \times 3 \text{ min/day} \times 365 \text{ days} \div 1440 \text{ min/day}$$

$$= \frac{438{,}000{,}000{,}000}{1{,}440} \approx 304{,}000{,}000 \text{ person-days}$$

## My estimate: **300,000,000 days**

- **China (~300M):** Traditional architecture used lattice/paper windows extensively. ~30% → **100M**
- **Japan (~30M):** Shoji screens/paper windows common. ~50% → **15M**
- **India (~200M):** Jharokha, jali, and various window openings. ~15% → **30M**
- **Americas (~30M with European-style buildings):** ~10M
- **Other regions:** ~25M

**Total with window access: ~340 million**

**Average daily window-gazing time:**
- ~90% agricultural laborers (outdoors most of the day): ~2 min/day
- ~8% urban workers/domestic: ~5 min/day
- ~2% wealthy/leisure class: ~15 min/day
- **Weighted average: ~2.5 minutes/day**

Note: People in 1800 were overwhelmingly outdoor laborers; windows were often small; artificial lighting was poor (reducing indoor time).

## Calculation

$$340{,}000{,}000 \text{ people} \times 2.5 \text{ min/day} \times 365 \text{ days/year} \div 1440 \text{ min/day}$$

$$= \frac{310{,}250{,}000{,}000}{1{,}440} \approx 215{,}500{,}000 \text{ person-days}$$

## My Estimate

**250,000,000 days**

(Rounding up slightly to account for uncertainty in window access across Asia and passive/incidental glancing.)

# Estimating Total Person-Days Spent Looking Through Windows in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~1 billion

**Fraction with meaningful window access (~30%):**

| Region | Population | % with lookable windows | People with access |
|---|---|---|---|
| Europe | 200M | 75% | 150M |
| China | 300M | 15% (paper windows are opaque; only open frames/wealthy) | 45M |
| India | 200M | 20% | 40M |
| Americas | 32M | 50% | 16M |
| Japan | 30M | 15% | 4.5M |
| Rest of world | 238M | 12% | ~29M |
| **Total** | | | **~285M** |

**Average daily window-gazing time (for those with access):**
- ~80% were agricultural laborers, mostly outdoors → ~1.5 min/day
- ~15% indoor/urban workers, artisans, domestic → ~5 min/day
- ~5% wealthy/leisured → ~15 min/day
- Children and elderly adjustments

**Weighted average: ~3 minutes/day** per person with access

## Calculation

$$285{,}000{,}000 \text{ people} \times 365 \text{ days} \times \frac{3 \text{ min}}{1440 \text{ min/day}}$$

$$= 285{,}000{,}000 \times 0.760 \approx 217{,}000{,}000 \text{ person-days}$$

## Sanity Check
This implies each of the 1 billion humans averaged ~0.22 days (≈5.2 hours) per year, or roughly 0.9 minutes/day including those without windows. That feels right for a predominantly outdoor-working, pre-industrial world population.

## My estimate: **220,000,000 person-days**

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~1 billion

**Fraction with meaningful window access (~30%):**
- Europe (~200M): ~60–70% had glass windows
- East Asia (~360M): paper/lattice windows in some homes, maybe 10–15%
- South Asia (~200M): very few glass windows, ~3–5%
- Africa (~100M), Americas, Middle East: generally low access
- **→ ~300 million people** with windows they could realistically gaze through

**Average daily time spent looking through a window (~4 minutes/day):**

| Group (among those with windows) | Share | Avg. time/day |
|---|---|---|
| Outdoor laborers/farmers | 50% | ~1 min |
| Indoor workers/craftsmen | 25% | ~4 min |
| Homebound (elderly, sick, domestic roles) | 15% | ~15 min |
| Children | 10% | ~3 min |

**Weighted average:** 0.5×1 + 0.25×4 + 0.15×15 + 0.10×3 ≈ **4 minutes/day**

## Calculation

$$300{,}000{,}000 \text{ people} \times 4 \text{ min/day} \times 365 \text{ days} = 438 \text{ billion person-minutes}$$

$$\frac{438{,}000{,}000{,}000}{1{,}440 \text{ min/day}} \approx 304{,}000{,}000 \text{ person-days}$$

## My Estimate

**300,000,000 person-days**

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~1 billion

**Fraction with access to windows:**
- Europe (~200M): ~70% had some form of window → 140M
- China (~300M): Paper/lattice windows common → ~50% → 150M
- India/South Asia (~200M): Open architecture, fewer enclosed windows → ~30% → 60M
- Rest of world (~300M): Highly variable → ~20% → 60M
- **Total with window access: ~410 million**

**Average daily window-gazing time (minutes/day):**

| Group | Population | Avg. gazing |
|---|---|---|
| Urban/wealthy/indoor workers | ~50M | ~8 min |
| Rural with decent windows | ~200M | ~2 min |
| Rural with basic openings | ~160M | ~1 min |
| No window access | ~590M | 0 min |

Most people were **agricultural laborers outdoors all day**. Indoor time was mostly sleeping (dark = no gazing) or working by dim light. Even when indoors during daytime, windows were small and tasks demanded attention.

## Calculation

**Daily total minutes:**
- 50M × 8 = 400M min
- 200M × 2 = 400M min
- 160M × 1 = 160M min
- **Total: 960 million minutes/day**

**Annual total:**
960M × 365 = 350.4 billion minutes/year

**Convert to person-days:**
350.4B ÷ 1,440 min/day ≈ **243 million person-days**

Rounding up slightly to account for uncertainty in gazing duration:

## My estimate: **350,000,000 days**

| Region | Population | % with windows | People with access |

| Europe | 195M | 65% | 127M |
| China | 330M | 25% | 82M |
| Indian subcontinent | 185M | 15% | 28M |
| Rest of Asia | 120M | 25% | 30M |
| Africa | 100M | 8% | 8M |
| Americas | 30M | 30% | 9M |
| **Total** | | | **~284M** |

I'll round to **300 million** with meaningful window access.

**Average daily time looking through a window:**
- Most people were agricultural laborers, outdoors dawn to dusk
- Indoor daylight time was limited (meals, breaks, domestic work)
- Women doing domestic work and children/elderly gazed more (~8–15 min/day)
- Male laborers less (~3–5 min/day)
- Wealthy/urban slightly more (~15–30 min/day)
- **Population-weighted average: ~6 minutes/day**

## Calculation

$$300{,}000{,}000 \times 365 \times \frac{6}{1440} \approx 456{,}000{,}000 \text{ person-days}$$

## My estimate: **450,000,000 person-days**

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

### World Population
~1 billion people in 1800.

### Fraction with Access to Windows
Not everyone had a "window" to look through:
- **Europe (~200M):** ~70% had glass or glazed windows → 140M
- **China/Japan (~330M):** ~55% had paper/lattice windows → ~180M
- **South Asia (~200M):** ~25% had window-like openings → 50M
- **Africa (~100M):** ~15% → 15M
- **Americas/Other (~170M):** ~35% → ~60M

**Total with window access: ~450 million**

### Average Daily Window-Gazing Time
Most people in 1800 were outdoor agricultural workers:

| Group (% of those with window access) | Awake indoor hours | Window-gazing |
|---|---|---|
| Outdoor laborers (60%) | ~5 hrs | ~2 min/day |
| Domestic/indoor workers (25%) | ~12 hrs | ~8 min/day |
| Children/elderly/sick (10%) | ~10 hrs | ~10 min/day |
| Wealthy/leisured (5%) | ~12 hrs | ~15 min/day |

**Weighted average ≈ 5 minutes/day** per person with window access.

### Calculation

$$450{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times 365 \;\text{days} \times \frac{5}{1440} \;\text{(days of gazing per day)} \approx 570{,}000{,}000$$

However, I think 5 min/day is at the upper end — windows were small, rooms dark, and "deliberately looking through" a window was rarer than today. Adjusting to **~4 min/day**:

$$450{,}000{,}000 \times 365 \times \frac{4}{1440} \approx 456{,}000{,}000$$

## My Best Estimate

**450,000,000 days**

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~1 billion

**Fraction with access to windows:**

| Region | Population | % with windows | People with windows |
|---|---|---|---|
| Western Europe | ~100M | ~85% | 85M |
| Eastern Europe/Russia | ~80M | ~60% | 48M |
| China | ~330M | ~50% (paper lattice windows common) | 165M |
| Japan/Korea | ~35M | ~60% (shoji screens) | 21M |
| South/SE Asia | ~250M | ~15% (tropical open dwellings) | 37M |
| Middle East/N. Africa | ~30M | ~40% | 12M |
| Sub-Saharan Africa | ~100M | ~10% | 10M |
| Americas | ~30M | ~40% | 12M |
| **Total** | | | **~390M** |

**Average daily window-gazing time (for those with windows): ~3.5 minutes/day**
- Most people were outdoor laborers (farmers); indoors only a few waking hours
- Glances to check weather, watch for visitors, idle moments near windows
- Elderly, children, urban dwellers, and wealthy skew higher (~5–15 min); rural laborers skew very low (~1–2 min)

## Calculation

$$390{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times \frac{3.5 \;\text{min/day}}{1440 \;\text{min/day}} \times 365 \;\text{days} \approx 345{,}000{,}000 \;\text{person-days}$$

## My estimate: **350,000,000 days**

The dominant factors keeping this relatively low: the overwhelmingly agrarian population spent most daylight hours outdoors, artificial lighting was poor (limiting evening gazing), and windows in much of the world were small or absent. Even generous assumptions about casual glances keep the per-person average well under 5 minutes/day.

- The vast majority were agricultural workers, spending 10+ hours outdoors daily

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~1 billion

**Fraction with access to see-through windows:**
- **Europe (~200M):** ~70% had glass windows → ~140M
- **China/Japan/Korea (~370M):** Paper windows (translucent, not transparent); only count when opened seasonally → effective ~50M
- **India, Middle East, Americas, etc. (~430M):** Open/shuttered/unglazed window openings in some fraction of dwellings → ~100M
- **Total effective window access:** ~300–350M → I'll use **~330M**

**Average daily window-gazing time (for those with window access):**
- ~70% were outdoor agricultural laborers: indoor waking time ~2 hrs → maybe **3–4 min/day** of window-gazing
- ~30% spent more time indoors (women doing domestic work, elderly, children, urban dwellers, artisans): **10–15 min/day**
- Weighted average: 0.7 × 3.5 + 0.3 × 12.5 ≈ **6 minutes/day**

## Calculation

$$330{,}000{,}000 \text{ people} \times 6 \text{ min/day} \times 365 \text{ days} = 722{,}700{,}000{,}000 \text{ person-minutes}$$

$$\frac{722{,}700{,}000{,}000}{1{,}440 \text{ min/day}} \approx 501{,}875{,}000 \text{ person-days}$$

### Adjustments downward:
- Infants/toddlers (~10% of window-havers) contribute negligibly
- Seasonal effects: in cold months, fewer open windows in non-glass regions; shorter days reduce usefulness
- Window tax (e.g., England) reduced window availability among the poor

After these adjustments (~10% reduction):

## **My estimate: 450,000,000 days**

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~1 billion

**Fraction with access to windows (broadly defined — glass, open lattice, shuttered openings):**

| Region | Population | % with window access | People with access |
|---|---|---|---|
| Europe | ~200M | ~95% | ~190M |
| East Asia (China, Japan, Korea) | ~350M | ~60% | ~210M |
| South/Southeast Asia | ~250M | ~30% | ~75M |
| Americas | ~30M | ~65% | ~20M |
| Middle East/N. Africa | ~40M | ~55% | ~22M |
| Sub-Saharan Africa | ~100M | ~15% | ~15M |
| **Total** | | | **~530M** |

Discounting for infants and very young children (~12%), effective population: **~450 million**

**Average daily window-gazing time:**
- ~80% were agricultural workers, spending most daylight hours outdoors → ~2 min/day
- ~15% mixed indoor work (domestic, craft, trade) → ~7 min/day
- ~5% wealthy/elderly/primarily indoor → ~15 min/day
- **Weighted average ≈ 3.5 minutes/day**

## Calculation

$$450{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times 3.5 \;\text{min/day} \times 365 \;\text{days} \div 1440 \;\text{min/day}$$

$$= \frac{450{,}000{,}000 \times 1{,}277.5}{1{,}440} \approx 399{,}000{,}000 \;\text{person-days}$$

## My estimate: **400,000,000 person-days**

This reflects the reality that in 1800, the vast majority of humanity spent most waking hours outdoors laboring, glass windows were largely a European luxury, and even indoor workers had little idle time for gazing. Window-looking was mostly brief and functional (checking weather, watching for visitors).

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~1 billion

**People with meaningful window access:**
| Region | Population | Window access rate | People with windows |
|--------|-----------|-------------------|-------------------|
| China (~330M) | Paper lattice windows very common even in peasant homes | ~75% | ~250M |
| Europe (~200M) | Glass windows widespread but many rural poor had only shuttered openings | ~65% | ~130M |
| India (~200M) | Small openings in the north, open-air in the south | ~35% | ~70M |
| Japan (~30M) | Shoji screens nearly universal | ~80% | ~25M |
| Americas, ME, Africa, etc. (~240M) | Highly variable | ~20% | ~50M |
| **Total** | | | **~525M** |

**Average daily window-gazing time:**
- Most people (subsistence farmers) spent 10+ hours outdoors; indoor time was for eating, sleeping, and household tasks in dim rooms with small windows
- Morning weather check: ~30–60 sec
- Occasional glances during indoor work: ~50–90 sec
- Watching for visitors/children: ~30 sec
- Elderly/sick/wealthy: significantly more (10–15 min)
- **Population-weighted average: ~3.25 minutes/day**

## Calculation

$$525{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times 365 \;\text{days} \times \frac{3.25}{1{,}440} \;\text{days of gazing per day}$$

$$= 525{,}000{,}000 \times 0.824 \approx 432{,}500{,}000$$

## My estimate: **430,000,000 person-days**

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing Days in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~1 billion

**Fraction with access to windows (any opening with a pane or covering designed for looking through):**

| Region | Population | Window access rate | People with access |
|---|---|---|---|
| Europe | 200M | 75% | 150M |
| China | 330M | 55% | 182M |
| Indian subcontinent | 200M | 35% | 70M |
| Japan/Korea | 35M | 60% | 21M |
| Middle East | 35M | 50% | 18M |
| Africa | 100M | 15% | 15M |
| Americas/Other | 100M | 25% | 25M |
| **Total** | | | **~450M** |

**Average daily window-gazing time** (for those with access), weighted by lifestyle:

- **Farmers/agricultural workers (~75%):** Outdoors most of the day, but household members (spouses, children) are indoors more. Average across household: ~3 min/day
- **Urban workers/craftsmen (~12%):** More indoor time: ~7 min/day
- **Wealthy/leisure class (~5%):** ~17 min/day
- **Elderly, infirm, young children indoors (~8%):** ~10 min/day

**Weighted average ≈ 4.7 minutes/day** for people with window access.

## Calculation

$$450{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times 365 \;\text{days} \times \frac{4.7}{1440} \;\text{(fraction of a day)} \approx 536{,}000{,}000 \;\text{person-days}$$

Cross-check: This means the average human on Earth (including those without windows) spent ~2.1 minutes/day looking through a window — which feels plausible.

## My estimate

**510,000,000 days**

# Estimating Total Window-Gazing Person-Days in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~1 billion

**Fraction with access to see-through windows:**
- **Europe (~200M):** Glass windows fairly widespread among middle/upper classes and in cities; rural poor often had shutters only. ~65% → **130M**
- **China (~330M):** Paper windows were common — translucent but *not* transparent (can't meaningfully "look through" them). Only open/unshuttered openings count. ~25% → **80M**
- **India (~200M):** Many dwellings had small openings; ~25% → **50M**
- **Americas (~30M):** Mixed. ~40% → **12M**
- **Japan, SE Asia, Middle East, Africa (~240M):** Varied architecture; open windows in warm climates, minimal glazing. ~25% → **60M**

**Total with functional see-through windows: ~330–400 million → I'll use 400M**

**Average daily time spent looking through a window:**
- ~75% rural agricultural workers: outdoors dawn-to-dusk; indoors only during darkness (can't see out). **~1 min/day**
- ~15% urban workers/artisans (often work indoors): occasional glances. **~5 min/day**
- ~10% wealthy, elderly, housebound, children at home: **~15 min/day**

**Weighted average: 0.75×1 + 0.15×5 + 0.10×15 = 3.0 minutes/day**

## Calculation

$$400{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times 3.0 \;\text{min/day} \times 365 \;\text{days} = 438{,}000{,}000{,}000 \;\text{person-minutes}$$

$$\frac{438{,}000{,}000{,}000}{1{,}440 \;\text{min/day}} \approx 304{,}000{,}000 \;\text{person-days}$$

## My estimate: **300,000,000 person-days**

# Estimating Total Person-Days Spent Looking Through Windows in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population (1800):** ~1 billion

**Fraction with access to transparent (glass) windows:**
- Europe & North America (~200M): Glass windows common among urban and middle/upper-class populations, but many rural poor had only shutters or tiny openings. Maybe ~50% had usable glass windows → ~100M
- East Asia (~400M): Paper/oiled-cloth windows were widespread — translucent, not transparent, so you can't really *look through* them. ~5% with glass → ~20M
- Rest of world (~400M): Glass windows very rare. ~5% → ~20M
- **Total with transparent windows: ~140–160 million people**

**Average daily window-gazing time:**
- Most people in 1800 were agricultural laborers spending daylight hours *outdoors*
- Even indoors, purposeful "looking through a window" (checking weather, watching streets, idle gazing) is brief
- Estimate: **~5 minutes/day** averaged across all people with window access (some days zero, some days more)

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~1 billion

**Fraction with meaningful window access (~35%):** ~350 million
- **Europe (~200M):** ~130M had window access (glass windows common among middle class and above; many rural poor had shuttered openings)
- **East Asia (~360M, China + Japan):** ~140M (paper/lattice windows were widespread in Chinese and Japanese architecture)
- **South/Southeast Asia (~250M):** ~37M (tropical open-air structures often lacked enclosed windows)
- **Americas (~30M):** ~10M
- **Middle East/N. Africa (~30M):** ~12M
- **Sub-Saharan Africa (~100M):** ~10M

**Average daily window-gazing time: ~4 minutes/day** for those with access
- ~80%+ of the population were outdoor agricultural workers — limited indoor time
- Indoor tasks (sewing, cooking, reading) were done near windows for light, but *looking through* a window is intermittent
- Includes deliberate gazing (~1–2 min) and incidental glancing (~2–3 min)
- Children, the elderly, and those in inclement weather gaze more; outdoor laborers far less

## Calculation

$$350{,}000{,}000 \text{ people} \times 365 \text{ days} \times 4 \text{ min/day} = 511 \text{ billion person-minutes}$$

$$\frac{511 \times 10^9}{1{,}440 \text{ min/day}} \approx 355{,}000{,}000 \text{ person-days}$$

## My estimate: **350,000,000 person-days**

- Children, infants — minimal.
- **Weighted average: ~5 minutes/day** (0.00347 days)

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

| Region | Population | % with windows | People with access |

| Indian subcontinent | ~200M | ~40% | 80M |
| Japan | ~30M | ~60% | 18M |

| Americas & other | ~150M | ~20% | 30M |
| **Total** | | | **~470 million** |

- Farmers/laborers (~70%): ~1 min/day (outdoors most of the day)

## My estimate: **300,000,000 person-days**

## Key Parameters
